sections.content:
What Is a Functional Group?

A functional group is a specific arrangement of atoms within an organic molecule that determines how that molecule reacts chemically. The carbon skeleton (hydrocarbon backbone) is largely inert — it is the functional groups attached to it that give each molecule its identity, reactivity, and biological role.

Think of functional groups as the verbs of chemistry: the carbon backbone is just scaffolding, but functional groups are what actually do things — donate protons, form esters, accept electrons, or participate in hydrogen bonding. Recognising a functional group tells you immediately what chemistry is possible.

The Major Functional Groups in Biology

Hydroxyl (–OH)

The hydroxyl group consists of an oxygen bonded to a hydrogen. It is highly polar and participates in hydrogen bonding, making molecules that carry it (alcohols) water-soluble. In biology, hydroxyl groups are found on sugars (e.g., glucose has five –OH groups), on the amino acid serine, threonine and tyrosine, and are frequent targets for phosphorylation during cell signalling.

Carbonyl (C=O)

A carbon double-bonded to oxygen is a carbonyl group. Its location determines whether the molecule is an aldehyde (carbonyl at the end of the chain, as in glucose in its open form) or a ketone (carbonyl in the middle, as in fructose). The C=O bond is strongly polarised — the carbon is electrophilic, making carbonyls reactive centres in condensation and addition reactions.

Carboxyl (–COOH)

A carboxyl group is a combination of a carbonyl and a hydroxyl on the same carbon. It can donate a proton (H⁺) to solution, acting as a weak acid (pKa ~4–5). Every amino acid has a carboxyl group, and fatty acids owe their acidic character to –COOH. In peptide bond formation, the carboxyl of one amino acid reacts with the amino group of the next, expelling water.

Amino (–NH₂)

The amino group contains nitrogen bonded to two hydrogens. It is a weak base — it can accept a proton to become –NH₃⁺. All amino acids carry an amino group (hence the name), and nucleobases in DNA and RNA also contain amino groups that form the hydrogen bonds responsible for base pairing.

🔑 Memory aid — the four core groups:
–OH (hydroxyl) → hydrogen bonds, water solubility
C=O (carbonyl) → reactive electrophilic carbon
–COOH (carboxyl) → acidic, releases H⁺
–NH₂ (amino) → basic, accepts H⁺

More Functional Groups You Will Encounter

Phosphate (–OPO₃²⁻)

The phosphate group carries two negative charges at physiological pH. It is the backbone of DNA and RNA, the energy-currency group in ATP, and the on/off switch in signal transduction (phosphorylation by kinases, dephosphorylation by phosphatases). The high-energy phosphoanhydride bonds in ATP release about 30 kJ/mol upon hydrolysis — energy that drives biosynthesis, active transport, and mechanical work.

Sulfhydryl (–SH)

Also called a thiol, the sulfhydryl group is found on the amino acid cysteine. Two –SH groups can be oxidised to form a disulfide bond (–S–S–), which covalently cross-links protein chains and is critical for the stability of secreted proteins (e.g., antibodies, insulin).

Methyl (–CH₃)

The methyl group is non-polar and does not participate in hydrogen bonding. However, its addition to DNA bases (DNA methylation) or histone tails (histone methylation) by methyltransferase enzymes is a key epigenetic mechanism that regulates gene expression without changing the underlying DNA sequence.

Functional Groups and Water Solubility

Whether a biological molecule dissolves in water or partitions into a lipid membrane depends almost entirely on its functional groups. Groups that can form hydrogen bonds with water — hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, phosphate — are hydrophilic. Non-polar groups — methyl, large hydrocarbon chains — are hydrophobic.

Molecules with both hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions are amphipathic. Phospholipids are the classic example: a charged phosphate head (hydrophilic) attached to two fatty acid tails (hydrophobic). In water, phospholipids spontaneously form a bilayer — the architecture of every biological membrane — because this arrangement maximises favourable interactions and minimises unfavourable ones.

Ionisation and pH

Many functional groups change their charge depending on the pH of their environment. The Henderson–Hasselbalch equation (pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA])) tells us the ratio of deprotonated to protonated form at any given pH. At physiological pH (≈7.4):

  • Carboxyl groups (pKa ≈4.5) are almost fully deprotonated: –COO⁻
  • Amino groups (pKa ≈9) are almost fully protonated: –NH₃⁺
  • Histidine's imidazole (pKa ≈6) can be either form near physiological pH — which is why histidine is so common in enzyme active sites

This pH-dependent ionisation is how enzymes titrate their active-site residues to precisely the charge state needed for catalysis.

🧪 Putting it together — reading a biomolecule: When you encounter an unfamiliar molecule, scan it systematically for functional groups. Each one tells a story: –OH groups suggest hydrogen bonding and enzyme recognition sites; –COOH suggests acidity and peptide bond potential; –NH₂ suggests basicity and nucleophilicity; phosphate suggests energy storage or nucleic acid backbone; –SH suggests a cysteine residue capable of disulfide bonding.

quiz.lessonSummary: Functional groups are specific arrangements of atoms that determine the chemical behaviour of organic molecules. The major groups in biology are: hydroxyl (–OH), carbonyl (C=O), carboxyl (–COOH), amino (–NH₂), phosphate (–OPO₃²⁻), sulfhydryl (–SH), and methyl (–CH₃). Each group confers distinct properties — acid/base behaviour, hydrogen bonding capacity, reactivity, and solubility. Understanding functional groups allows you to predict how molecules will behave, interact, and react in biological systems.

questions.question: Amino acids are the building blocks of proteins. Every amino acid has both an amino group (–NH₂) and a carboxyl group (–COOH). Explain how the acid–base properties of these two functional groups determine the overall charge of an amino acid at physiological pH (7.4), and why this matters for how amino acids behave in an aqueous cellular environment.

ATP (adenosine triphosphate) is the primary energy currency of the cell. Its energy storage depends on the phosphate functional groups it contains. Describe the chemical features of the phosphate group that make it suitable for energy storage and transfer, and explain what happens chemically when ATP is hydrolysed to ADP and inorganic phosphate.

Phospholipids are described as amphipathic molecules. Using your knowledge of functional groups, explain what structural features make phospholipids amphipathic, and describe how this property drives the spontaneous formation of the lipid bilayer — the fundamental architecture of biological membranes.

DNA methylation involves the addition of a methyl group (–CH₃) to cytosine bases in DNA. Given that the methyl group is non-polar and does not participate in hydrogen bonding or change the base-pairing rules of DNA, propose a mechanism by which methyl groups could still influence gene expression. What properties of the methyl group might allow it to affect how proteins interact with DNA?
